sections.heading: Clinical Note
sections.section_value:
Chief Complaint
Recurrent lung infections and persistent symptoms.
History of Present Illness
The patient reports a history of frequent lower respiratory infections over the past six months, with symptoms including persistent cough, mucus production, shortness of breath, and low-grade fever. He attributes these symptoms to his work helping his brother with hay unloading. He notes that his symptoms consistently worsen when engaging in this activity. He has sought treatment from his primary care physician and urgent care multiple times, receiving antibiotics and experiencing temporary relief. He reports a family history of seasonal allergies but denies personal experience with them. He also mentions a large family (ten siblings).
Findings
Vitals reveal a respiratory rate of 20 breaths per minute and oxygen saturation of 99% on room air. The chest x-ray demonstrates round glass opacities in the lungs. Physical exam reveals fine rales in the lungs but no wheezes. Heart sounds are regular. Clubbing of the fingers is not present. The patient reports no murmurs, rubs, or gallops.
Assessment
Based on the patient's history of recurrent lung infections, particularly associated with hay unloading, and the presence of round glass opacities on chest x-ray, a diagnosis of hypersensitivity pneumonitis (farmer's lung) is suspected.
Plan

sections.heading: Clinical Note
sections.section_value: Prescribe a course of oral steroids to reduce inflammation in the lungs. Order a CT scan of the lungs to confirm the diagnosis and assess the extent of lung damage. Order a pulmonary function test to evaluate respiratory impairment. Strongly recommend eliminating exposure to hay or using a respirator when working with hay to prevent further lung damage. Schedule a follow-up appointment in four weeks to assess response to treatment and adjust management as needed. Advise the patient to involve more siblings in work to reduce his exposure to hay.
